coredata.dc:title: Interface engineering of LaSrCoO-N-CDs-FeOOH composites for synergistic water-splitting catalysis

coredata.dc:description: Efficient and durable electrocatalysts are essential for sustainable water splitting. Here, we present a La0.5Sr0.5CoO3 (LSC)-N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)-FeOOH composite synthesized via wet ball milling. N-CDs act as electron bridges, enhancing charge transfer and inducing partial amorphization of FeOOH, which improves catalytic activity. The optimized composite achieves low overpotentials of 295 mV and 308 mV for OER and HER at 10 mA/cm2 in 1.0 M KOH aqueous solution, with reduced charge transfer resistance and increased active surface area. Long-term testing at 500 mA/cm2 for 230 hours demonstrated exceptional stability. These results establish LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH as a promising candidate for high-performance water-splitting electrocatalysts.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic representation of the synthesis process for LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH nanoparticles (NPs). (b) SEM images of LSC nanoparticles with scale bars of 500 nm (left) and 100 nm (right). (c) SEM images of FeOOH nanoparticles with scale bars of 500 nm (left) and 100 nm (right). (d) SEM images of LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH nanoparticles with scale bars of 500 nm (left) and 100 nm (right) for (b), (c), and (d), respectively.

(a) TEM images and corresponding selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns showing the mapping region and elements La, O, Fe, N, and C. (b) Low-magnification TEM images of LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH. (c-d) High-magnification images and Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) patterns of the LSC/FeOOH interfaces. (e) High-magnification image of the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH interface. (f) FFT pattern of FeOOH away from the N-CDs. (g) FFT pattern of FeOOH near the N-CDs. The scale bars are 200 nm in (a) and (b), and 5 nm−1 in (d), (f), and (g).
X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of LSC, FeOOH, and LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH. Each panel corresponds to the binding energy range of (a) La 3d, (b) Co 2p, (c) Fe 2p, and (d) O 1 s.
Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy of FeOOH, LSC, LSC-FeOOH, LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH. (a) UPS VB region, (b) UPS secondary electron region, and (c) Tauc plot from Uv-vis absorption.

Electrochemical performances of LSC, FeOOH, LSC-FeOOH, LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH, Pt/C, and IrO2 electrode. (a) OER polarization curves of various catalysts in 1 M KOH (b) calculated Tafel slopes for each electrode in OER. (c) HER polarization curves of various catalysts (d) calculated Tafel slopes for each electrode in HER. (e) Nyquist plots of LSC, FeOOH, LSC-FeOOH, LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH (f) electrochemical double-layer capacity (Cdl) of LSC, FeOOH, LSC-FeOOH, and LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH. All the analysis are conducted against Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode in 1 M KOH saturated N2 gas.

(a) Chronopotentiometric stability test of LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH, and IrO2 measured at 500 mA/cm2. (b) SEM images of LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH coated on nickel foam in before and after chronopotentiometric stability test. The scale bars are 100 μm(top), and 500 nm(bottom) (c) Comparison of long-term stability time and current density of various reported composite electrocatalyst in alkaline electrolytes. (d) X-ray photoelectron spectra of LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH of before and after stability test. Each panel is corresponding binding energy range to La 3d, Fe 2p, and O 1 s.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficient and durable electrocatalysts are essential for sustainable water splitting. Here, we present a La0.5Sr0.5CoO3 (LSC)-N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)-FeOOH composite synthesized via wet ball milling. N-CDs act as electron bridges, enhancing charge transfer and inducing partial amorphization of FeOOH, which improves catalytic activity. The optimized composite achieves low overpotentials of 295 mV and 308 mV for OER and HER at 10 mA/cm2 in 1.0 M KOH aqueous solution, with reduced charge transfer resistance and increased active surface area. Long-term testing at 500 mA/cm2 for 230 hours demonstrated exceptional stability. These results establish LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH as a promising candidate for high-performance water-splitting electrocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen is widely recognized as a clean and sustainable energy carrier with the potential to play a crucial role in tackling global energy challenges [1,2]. In contrast to fossil fuels, hydrogen combustion generates only water as a byproduct, making it an eco-friendly alternative to traditional energy sources. With its high efficient energy density, hydrogen is particularly suitable for applications in transportation, energy storage, and various industrial processes. Moreover, as the world transitions toward carbon neutrality, hydrogen is expected to serve as a crucial bridge between renewable energy sources and applications, providing a reliable means of storing and transporting energy [3,4]. One of the most promising methods for hydrogen production is water electrolysis, a process that separates water molecules into hydrogen and oxygen through an electrochemical reaction. This technique can be powered by renewable energy sources, like solar or wind, to enable genuinely sustainable hydrogen production. Among the two half-reactions in water electrolysis, the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) at the cathode and the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) at the anode, the OER presents significant challenges due to its inherently sluggish kinetics and high energy barriers [5–7]. Addressing these challenges is key to improving the overall efficiency and economic viability of hydrogen production via water splitting.

Recent advances in OER catalysts have focused on transition metal-based materials due to their exceptional activity and stability in alkaline media [8–14]. For instance, sulfur-doped CoO on Co3O4 maintained stability for 20 hours at 10 mA/cm2, attributed to surface reconstruction that enhances Co3+/Co2+ redox activity. Similarly, NiO@NiMoO4 composites showed superior long-term stability through synergistic effects at the NiO-NiMoO4 interface, which improved electron transfer and Ni ion electrophilicity, facilitating hydroxide adsorption/desorption for enhanced OER performance. NiFe layered double hydroxides (NiFe-LDH) supported on carbon spheres also demonstrated potential, offering a large surface area of 110.2 m2/g and achieving a reduced overpotential of 292 mV at 50 mA/cm2. Despite these advancements, transition metal-based catalysts face challenges such as environmental concerns, low intrinsic conductivity, and structural instability under high current densities, which limit their long-term stability and practical applicability in OER processes [10,12,15,16].

Among transition metal-based materials, oxyhydroxides such as FeOOH have garnered significant attention as catalysts for the OER due to their exceptional material properties. Notably, the Fe3+/Fe4+ redox couple in FeOOH is highly compatible with oxygen evolution under alkaline conditions, promoting efficient charge transfer and facilitating the formation of oxygen intermediates [17–20]. Moreover, the abundant surface hydroxyl groups and layered structure of FeOOH provide a high density of active sites, contributing to its superior catalytic performance [19–23]. Despite these advantages, FeOOH faces several critical challenges that limit its practical application in water electrolysis. One major limitation is its intrinsic low electrical conductivity, which hinders efficient charge transfer during the OER [24,25]. Additionally, FeOOH exhibits insufficient long-term stability under harsh electrochemical conditions, such as high current densities and extended operational periods, leading to significant performance degradation over time [26]. These challenges collectively reduce the catalytic efficiency and durability of FeOOH, posing obstacles for its scalability in hydrogen production. To address these issues, various strategies have been explored to enhance the electrocatalytic performance of FeOOH. Integrating FeOOH with highly conductive supports, such as carbon-based materials or perovskite oxides, has been shown to improve electron transport during the reaction [27–29]. Doping FeOOH with elements like cobalt, nickel, or molybdenum effectively tunes its electronic structure and increases the availability of active sites [30–32]. Additionally, structural modifications, including the synthesis of nanostructured FeOOH or the creation of heterostructures, has demonstrated significant potential to boost its catalytic activity and durability [33–35].

To fully realize the potential of FeOOH and similar materials as efficient and durable OER catalysts, it is essential to not only enhance their intrinsic properties but also explore the role of interfaces in catalytic systems. The interfaces between catalysts and their supports have emerged as critical factors that significantly influence catalytic performance. At these interfaces, unique phenomena such as electronic interactions, lattice distortions, and charge transfer processes occur, profoundly impacting both the catalytic performance and the long-term durability of the material [36–38]. For instance, Fe-doped Ni oxyhydroxides embedded within carbon exhibit enhanced OER activity, primarily due to strong interfacial interactions that promote electron transfer and increase the exposure of active Fe sites [39]. Similarly, MoS2@Ni3S2 heteronanorods demonstrate improved bifunctional electrochemical performance, attributed to their unique heterostructure. The interface between MoS2 and Ni3S2 facilitates efficient charge transfer and provides abundant active sites, thereby boosting overall water-splitting activity [40]. These interfacial regions also act as hotspots for reaction processes, where the interaction between the catalyst and its support can either improve or limit the overall efficiency. Thus, understanding the electronic and atomic structures of these interfaces is crucial for uncovering the mechanisms behind their performance [41–44]. By optimizing interfacial properties through material design and advanced characterization techniques, it is possible to develop next-generation OER catalysts with improved performance and durability.

Herein, we report a La0.5Sr0.5CoO3 (LSC)-N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs)-FeOOH composite synthesized via a two-step wet ball milling process, demonstrating exceptional electrocatalytic performance and stability for water splitting applications. The heterostructure integrates perovskite oxide LSC, rod-like FeOOH, and ultrasmall N-CDs to synergistically enhance electron transfer, lower charge transfer resistance, and increase the active surface area. N-CDs serve as electron bridges, inducing lattice distortions in FeOOH and facilitating partial amorphization, thereby improving catalytic activity. The optimized composite achieved low overpotentials of 295 mV and 308 mV for OER and HER at 10 mA/cm2, with enhanced reaction kinetics and superior performance even at high current densities. Long-term stability testing at 500 mA/cm2 over 230 hours demonstrated remarkable durability, with no structural or chemical degradation. These findings underscore the critical role of N-CDs and FeOOH in enhancing the electrochemical properties of LSC, positioning the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composite as a highly efficient and durable electrocatalyst for next-generation water-splitting technologies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of LSC, N-CDs, and FeOOH
ce:section.ce:para: Lanthanum chloride heptahydrate (LaCl3·7 H2O), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2·6 H2O), strontium chloride hexahydrate (SrCl2·6 H2O), citric acid, iron chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6 H2O), nitric acid solution (HNO3, 70 %), dopamine hydrochloride, anhydrous ethyl alcohol (ethanol, 99.5 %), and potassium hydroxide solution (KOH, 45 %) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich.

The LSC was synthesized using the Pechini method. In a three-neck flask, 5 mmol of LaCl3·7H₂O, 5 mmol of SrCl2·6H₂O, 10 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6 H2O, and 20 mmol of citric acid were combined with 1 mL of HNO3 under continuous stirring. Once the materials were completely dissolved, 15 mL of ethylene glycol was added, and the mixture was heated until a gel-like consistency was achieved. The gel was then calcined at 700°C for 12 hours in air, followed by washing with deionized water and ethanol three times.

N-CDs were synthesized using hydrothermal method. A mixture of 0.125 g of dopamine hydrochloride and 0.5 g of citric acid was dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol. This solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated to 180°C for 6 hours in an oven. After cooling naturally, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 minutes, and the process was repeated three times. The supernatant was discarded, and the product was filtered using a 0.22 μm syringe membrane filter.

FeOOH was prepared using a hydrolysis method. A 6 mmol solution of FeCl₃·6H₂O in 150 mL of deionized water was stirred at 80°C for 15 hours. The resulting suspension was washed with deionized water and ethanol three times and then dried in an oven at 60°C for 12 hours.

To reduce the particle size of LSC, the synthesized powders were subjected to high-energy ball milling in ethanol for 12 hours using stainless-steel balls. N-CDs and FeOOH were then added to the milled LSC at varying weight ratios (10 wt%, 20 wt%, 30 wt%, and 40 wt%), followed by sonication. To optimize the ratio of LSC to FeOOH, mixtures with different ratios (1:1, 2:1, and 1:2) were milled for 0.5 hours. The resulting composite powders were collected by centrifugation at 13,000 rpm for 10 minutes, followed by drying overnight in a vacuum oven at 60°C. This composite material was labeled as LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH.

The samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer equipped with Ni Kα radiation. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) images and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) mapping were conducted on a JSM-IT800 system operating at 15 kV. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning transmission electron microscopy-energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (STEM-EDS) were performed using a JEM-ARM200F transmission electron microscope. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy were carried out using a PHI 5000 VersaProbe (Ulvac-PHI, Japan) with Al Kα radiation. Ultraviolet–visible molecular absorption spectroscopy(UV–vis) was implemented using Cary 5000.

Electrochemical performance was evaluated with the Ivium-n-Stat system in a standard three-electrode setup. The working electrode was a glassy carbon (GC) electrode (2 mm diameter), with a Pt mesh (3 × 3 cm2) as the counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl electrode (saturated KCl solution) as the reference. A catalyst ink was prepared by sonicating 10 mg of the electrocatalyst (LSC, FeOOH, LSC-FeOOH, LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH, and IrO2 from Premetek Co) in 1 mL of a binder solution (45:45:10 = ethanol:isopropyl alcohol:5 wt% nafion solution from Sigma-Aldrich) for 20 minutes. 5 μL of the suspension was carefully dropped onto the polished GC electrode and dried at room temperature, resulting in a loading mass of 0.1 mg. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was performed in a 1.0 M KOH solution with potential ranges of 0–0.8 V vs. Ag/AgCl for the OER and −0.8 to −1.8 V vs. Ag/AgCl for the HER at a scan rate of 50 mV/s and a rotation speed of 700 rpm. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out with a 10 mV amplitude over a frequency range of 1–10 kHz to assess charge transfer resistance. Double-layer capacitance (Cdl) was determined from cyclic voltammetry curves obtained at scan rates of 10, 20, 50, 100, and 200 mV/s.

For long-term stability testing, a two-electrode configuration was used in which Ni foam as both the anode and cathode. The Ni foam was pretreated by immersing it in a 30 % HCl solution for 10 minutes followed by bath sonication, and then activated by sonication in acetone for 5 minutes, repeated three times. Chronopotentiometry (CP) measurements were conducted at a current density of 500 mA/cm2 for 230 hours in a 1.0 M KOH solution with a rotation speed of 700 rpm, using the Ivium-n-Stat system.

Fig. 1a illustrates the schematic diagram for synthesizing LSC, FeOOH, N-CDs, and LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH powders using the wet ball milling method. LSC was synthesized via the sol-gel method and then subjected to high-energy ball milling for 12 hours to decrease the particle size. Before ball milling, the LSC nanoparticles exhibited a cubic shape with an average size of approximately 200 nm (Figure S1). After ball milling, the particles were reduced to an average size of approximately 43 nm (Fig. 1b). FeOOH was prepared via a direct hydrolysis method with continuous stirring at 80°C for 15 hours. FE-SEM images of the synthesized FeOOH revealed uniform rod-shaped particles with an average length of approximately 120 nm and a width of around 50 nm (Fig. 1c). N-CDs were produced through a hydrothermal method using a mixture of dopamine hydrochloride and citric acid at 180°C for 6 hours. Uniform particle sizes of approximately 2 nm and good crystallinity were observed in the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of as-synthesized N-CDs. The lattice fringes corresponded to the (100) plane, with a lattice spacing of 0.210 nm, as shown in Figure S2. The LSC and N-CDs were then combined and subjected to ball milling for an additional 30 minutes. The weight ratios of FeOOH and N-CDs were adjusted to optimize conditions for the best electrochemical performance. LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composites were prepared by first ball milling LSC and N-CDs at specific weight ratios (10–40 wt%), followed by a second ball milling step with FeOOH at ratios of 1:1, 2:1, and 1:2. The resulting composites were collected by centrifugation at 13,000 rpm for 10 minutes and subsequently dried in a vacuum oven at 60°C overnight to eliminate any residual moisture. Fig. 1d shows the SEM image of the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composites, where some ultra-small N-CD particles, approximately 3 nm in size, were observed between the LSC and FeOOH.

The crystalline structures of LSC, FeOOH and LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composites were examined using X-ray diffraction (XRD), as shown in Figure S3. The LSC nanoparticles exhibit the most intense diffraction peaks at 33°, 47°, and 59°, which correspond to the (104), (024), and (214) planes, as identified by JCPDS Card No. 36–1392. Meanwhile, the pristine FeOOH displays a pattern characteristic of the β-FeOOH phase, with the most prominent peaks observed at 27°, 35°, and 56°, corresponding to the (310), (211), and (600) planes, as referenced by JCPDS Card No. 34–1226. In contrast, the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composite exhibits a more complex pattern, with peaks corresponding to both FeOOH and the perovskite LSC structure, indicating successful integration of these components. The retention of distinct crystalline peaks for FeOOH and LSC suggests that the ball milling process preserved their structural integrity while forming a hybrid composite. However, the FeOOH peaks in the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composite are noticeably broader compared to those of pristine FeOOH, indicating a partial decrease in crystallinity in the FeOOH phase. This broadening may be attributed to lattice distortions or reduced grain size introduced during the ball milling process and the incorporation of N-CDs, which can disrupt the long-range order of the FeOOH lattice.

For advanced morphological analysis, HR-TEM combined with elemental analysis was performed, as shown in Figs. S4, 2, and S5. The high-magnification image in Figure S4a reveals that β-FeOOH exhibited good crystallinity, as evidenced by lattice-plane spacings of 0.25 nm and 0.33 nm, corresponding to the β-FeOOH (110) and (310) planes, respectively. The fast Fourier transform (FFT) patterns further confirm that the FCC structure of β-FeOOH was well-formed. The HR-TEM image of LSC also demonstrates its superior crystallinity, with a lattice-plane spacing of 0.237 nm corresponding to the La0.5Sr0.5CoO3 (113) plane, as shown in Figure S4b. In Fig. 2a, X-ray spectroscopy (STEM-EDS) mapping of the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composites reveals a well-dispersed elemental distribution of La, O, Fe, N, and C, indicating that the LSC was effectively functionalized with N-CDs, coated with FeOOH. At high magnification in Fig. 2b, ultrasmall N-CDs with sizes ranging from 2 to 3 nm were observed at the interface between LSC and FeOOH. Although proving the presence of N-CDs at the interface is challenging, the high-magnification image clearly shows a distinct lattice fringe of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of N-CDs, which confirms their existence at the interface (Figure S5).

The interface between FeOOH and LSCO was further investigated using HR-TEM to elucidate structural characteristics and material interactions (Fig. 2c-g). As shown in Figs. 2c and 2d, the LSCO region far from the N-CDs exhibits well-defined lattice fringes with a spacing of approximately 0.27 nm, corresponding to the (110) crystallographic plane. Adjacent to LSCO, crystalline FeOOH displays lattice spacings of 0.32 nm and 0.22 nm, indexed to the (310) and (031) planes, respectively, forming an angle of ∼72°. This structural coherence suggests a stable interface between LSCO and crystalline FeOOH, confirmed by the FFT analysis in Fig. 2d. This indicates that the ball milling process does not damage the material or introduce defects. In contrast, the region near the N-CDs is observed to transform into an amorphous FeOOH region, confirmed by the HR image and FFT analysis.(Fig. 2e-g) The transition from crystalline to amorphous FeOOH may suggest structural disorganization, driven by the combined effects of N-CDs and ball milling. N-CDs induce lattice strain and weaken the crystal structure through electron transfer and chemical bonding, while ball milling introduces mechanical forces that exacerbate these disruptions. Specifically, electron transfer from N-CDs to FeOOH may play a key role in destabilizing the crystalline lattice. The transfer of electrons alters the oxidation state of Fe atoms (e.g., Fe3+ to Fe2+), resulting in lattice distortions due to differences in ionic radii [45]. These distortions accumulate near the N-CDs, leading to the formation of disordered, amorphous regions in FeOOH (Fig. 2g). Additionally, the high-energy impacts during ball milling create defects and enhance structural disorder, further promoting amorphization [46]. Together, the synergistic effects of N-CDs and ball milling effectively disrupt the long-range order of FeOOH, driving the transition to an amorphous phase [47–49].

X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) was used to examine the chemical and electronic states, helping to elucidate the interface interactions. Fig. 3 presents the La 3d, Co 2p, Fe 2p, and O 1 s core-level spectra of the LSC, FeOOH, and LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH samples. In Fig. 3a, the peaks at 833.77 eV and 850.75 eV in pristine LSC correspond to the La 3d 5/2 and La 3d 3/2 doublets of the La3+ oxidation state, with strong shake-up satellite peaks at 837.55 eV and 854.56 eV, respectively. After forming the composites, the La 3d peaks shifted by 1.7 eV and 1.4 eV towards higher binding energies in the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composites, respectively. This shift suggests that La has undergone a transition to a higher oxidation state, reflecting electron withdrawal from the La atoms. Fig. 3b shows that the Co 2p spectrum consists of two spin-orbit pairs, Co 2p 3/2 and Co 2p 1/2, corresponding to Co3+ and Co2+, each accompanied by two satellite peaks. In pristine LSC, the Co3+ and Co2+ peaks are observed at 780.06/794.91 eV and 782.55/797.02 eV, respectively. In the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composite, these peaks shift to 781.46/796.07 eV for Co3+ and 784.80/798.35 eV for Co2+, resulting in a binding energy upshift of ∼1.4 eV for Co3+ and ∼2.34 eV for Co2+ compared to pristine LSC and LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH, which is consistent with the shift observed in the La 3d peaks. By comparing the Co 2p spectra volume fractions, the Co3+/(Co2+ + Co3+) ratios for LSC and LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH were found to be 0.47 and 0.49, respectively, indicating a higher proportion of Co3+ species in the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composite. This suggests that the Co 2p spectrum of LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH reflects higher oxidation states due to stronger electronic coupling between the LSC and N-CDs interface, which enhances electron transfer from LSC to the N-CDs.

Fig. 3c shows the XPS spectra of Fe 2p, which consists of two spin-orbit pairs: Fe 2p 3/2 (Fe3+ and Fe2+) and Fe 2p 1/2, each with one satellite peak. In the Fe 2p 3/2 spectrum of pristine FeOOH, Fe2⁺ and Fe3⁺ are observed at 710.36 eV and 712.0 eV, respectively. For the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composite, these peaks shift to 710.66 eV and 712.2 eV, indicating a binding energy upshift of ∼0.3 eV for Fe2⁺ and ∼0.2 eV for Fe3⁺ compared to pristine FeOOH, which is consistent with the shifts observed in the La 3d and Co 2p peaks. Additionally, the satellite peak intensity in the Fe 2p spectrum of LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH is reduced compared to pristine FeOOH. When comparing the Fe 2p 3/2 spectra volume fractions, the Fe2+/(Fe2+ + Fe3+) ratios for LSC and LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH were found to be 0.52 and 0.57, respectively, indicating a predominance of lower oxidation Fe2+ species in the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composite. These results suggest that electrons from the oxidized La and Co species in LSC are transferred to Fe in FeOOH, causing a partial reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+, with the N-CDs enhancing electron transfer within the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composite.

Finally, the O 1 s core-level spectra were examined to study the surface oxygen species of the catalysts, as they play a crucial role in determining electrochemical performance. In pristine LSC, the high-resolution O 1 s XPS spectrum exhibits four prominent peaks at 528.30, 529.23, 531.34, and 533.41 eV, representing lattice oxygen (M-O-M), highly oxidative oxygen (M-O), surface-active oxygen (OH-), and adsorbed water (H2O), respectively. The ratio of surface-active oxygen to lattice oxygen serves as an indicator for evaluating performance. Upon introducing N-CDs-FeOOH, an increase in surface-active oxygen was detected, suggesting enhanced adsorption of hydroxide species, which in turn improves the intrinsic electrochemical performance. A general shift in the core-level spectra of La 3d, Co 2p, Fe 2p, and O 1 s was observed following the introduction of N-CDs-FeOOH.

To further substantiate the proposed charge transfer mechanism and interfacial interaction, ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS), Tauc plot from UV–vis absorption, and Raman spectroscopy were conducted to investigate the interfacial electronic structure, as shown in Fig. 4, S6. UPS-derived valence band spectra revealed a gradual upward shift in the valence band maximum (VBM), measured as 3.851 eV for LSC, 1.914 eV for FeOOH, 3.034 eV for LSC–FeOOH, and 2.462 eV for LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH relative to the Fermi level, indicating interfacial band realignment that favors charge redistribution (Fig. 4a). Corresponding work function values, calculated from the secondary electron cutoff, were 3.443 eV, 4.318 eV, 3.838 eV, and 3.435 eV, respectively, demonstrating a reduction upon composite formation that suggests the presence of a built-in electric field across the interface (Fig. 4b). Optical band gaps estimated from UV–vis absorption spectra were 4.935 eV (LSC), 2.581 eV (FeOOH), 4.87 eV (LSC-FeOOH), and 4.85 eV (LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH), confirming that bulk electronic properties are largely preserved while interfacial levels are selectively tuned (Fig. 4c).

The band alignment of FeOOH, LSC, LSC-FeOOH, and LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH was established based on UPS and UV–vis absorption measurements, as shown in Figure S9. The Fermi levels, derived from the measured work functions, were positioned at −4.318 eV for FeOOH, −3.443 eV for LSC, −3.838 eV for LSC-FeOOH, and −3.435 eV for LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH. The corresponding VBM were found at −6.232 eV, −7.294, −6.872 eV, and −5.897 eV, while the CBM, estimated from optical band gaps of 2.581 eV, 4.935 eV, 4.87 eV, and 4.85 eV, were calculated to be −3.651 eV, −2.359 eV, −2.002 eV, and −1.047 eV, respectively. These results indicate a gradual upward shift in both the Fermi level and VBM across the heterostructure, along with a narrowing bandgap in the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composite [50–52]. The variation in work function and band edge positions suggests the formation of a built-in electric field at the LSC/N-CD/FeOOH interface, which facilitates spontaneous electron transfer from LSC to FeOOH. This interfacial charge redistribution and band bending are expected to enhance charge separation and carrier mobility, thereby contributing to the improved electrocatalytic activity observed in the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH system.

Figure S6 shows the Raman spectra of pristine N-CDs and the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composite, providing insights into the structural characteristics and degree of disorder in the carbon components. In pristine N-CDs, it exhibits two characteristic peaks near 1365 cm−1 (D band) and 1573 cm−1 (G band), corresponding to the disordered and graphitic sp2 carbon structures, respectively. The D band is associated with structural defects and edge distortions in the carbon lattice, while the G band reflects the E2 g vibrational mode of ordered sp2 carbon atoms [53,54]. In the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composite, both the D and G bands exhibit a lower shift to 1337 and 1545 cm−1, respectively. The downward shift suggests that electrons are partially transferred from N-CDs to the adjacent FeOOH domains [53]. This weakens the C-C bond force constants in the sp2 carbon lattice, leading to a softening of the vibrational modes and thus a lower shift. These analyses suggest that boosted charge transport aligns with the energy band configuration at the interface of LSC, N-CDs, and FeOOH (Figure S9).

The electrochemical performance of the synthesized LSC, LSC-FeOOH, and LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH heterostructures was evaluated by coating them onto a glassy carbon electrode, as shown in Fig. 5. Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was carried out under alkaline conditions (1.0 M KOH, pH = 13.2) using a three-electrode system with a scan rate of 50 mV/s. Initially, the OER performance was investigated by obtaining LSV curves at a scan rate of 50 mV/s, referenced to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) scale, in a 1.0 M KOH solution, as shown in Fig. 5a. The Tafel plots corresponding to the LSV curves in Fig. 5b were derived. For reference, a commercial IrO2/C sample demonstrated excellent OER performance with an overpotential of 240 mV vs. RHE and a Tafel slope of 54 mV/dec, comparable to that of commercial catalysts. In LSC sample, the overpotential and the Tafel slope of LSC increased to 330 mV vs. RHE at 10 mA/cm2 and 84.4 mV/dec, respectively. Simultaneously, the pristine FeOOH had the overpotential of 434 mV vs. RHE at 10 mA/cm2 and the Tafel slope of 61.8 mV/dec. When the composites were prepared by using LSC and FeOOH, they decreased to 312 mV and 53.5 mV/dec, respectively. Finally, the OER performance of the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH heterostructures was assessed under the same conditions. To determine the optimum weight ratio of N-CDs, the concentration was varied from 10 to 30 wt%, and OER performance was tested, as shown in Figure S7. The 10 wt% sample exhibited the lowest overpotential of 295 mV at 10 mA/cm2. However, at a high current density of 200 mA/cm2, the 20 wt% sample showed a lower overpotential of 456 mV, with a Tafel slope of 50.2 mV/dec, which is smaller than that of any other sample, including the IrO2 catalyst. Further increases in N-CD content significantly degraded OER performance due to excessive N-CD coverage, which blocked the active sites of LSC and FeOOH.

The HER performance of LSC, FeOOH, LSC-FeOOH, and LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH heterostructures was also tested in a 1.0 M KOH electrolyte, as shown in Fig. 5c. To evaluate the electrocatalytic activity for HER, a Pt wire was used as the counter electrode, and the current density versus potential was measured at a scan rate of 50 mV/s at room temperature. For pristine LSC and FeOOH, a current density of −10 mA/cm2 was obtained at overpotentials of 398 mV and 451 mV, with Tafel slopes of 124 mV/dec and 184 mV/dec, respectively. When LSC and FeOOH were combined into composites, these values decreased to 333 mV and 117 mV/dec, respectively. The concentration of N-CDs was optimized by evaluating HER performance, showing the lowest overpotential of 308 mV and a Tafel slope of 110 mV/dec for the 20 wt% sample. Although commercial Pt/C showed the lowest overpotential and a Tafel slope of 158 mV/dec, the composites exhibited a lower Tafel slope, indicating that a lower voltage is needed to achieve a high current density. At a higher current density of 150 mA/cm2, the 20 wt% sample displayed a lower overpotential. The improvement in electrochemical performance can be attributed to charge transfer facilitated by the work function difference between FeOOH and the perovskite oxide, leading to modifications in the occupancy of the t2 g and eg orbitals (Figure S9) [55–57]. The high electronegativity of FeOOH likely alters the electronic structure of Co by reducing the electron density around it, particularly in the eg occupancy. This is significant because a σ∗-orbital occupancy near 1 in perovskite oxides increases the binding affinity of oxygen species at the B-site, thus enhancing OER and HER performance. Furthermore, the increased ratio of Co species with higher oxidation states correlates with an eg-orbital filling near 1, a key factor for effective bifunctional catalysis. These structural features collectively create a built-in electric field at the LSC-FeOOH interface and facilitate directional charge redistribution. This interfacial interaction induces a shift in the oxidation states of Co and Fe toward catalytically favorable Co3+ and Fe2+ species, as confirmed by XPS analysis and supported by band alignment. At the interface of the perovskite oxide and FeOOH, FeOOH likely influences the coordination modes of anions, facilitating the hydroxyl adsorption step in the lattice oxygen evolution reaction. This synergistic interaction at the interface accelerates the reaction kinetics, further enhancing OER and HER performance [58]. Furthermore, the amorphous FeOOH phase contributes significantly to the enhanced electrocatalytic performance of the composite. Unlike its crystalline counterpart, amorphous FeOOH possesses a higher density of unsaturated coordination sites, greater structural flexibility, and more accessible defect sites [59,60]. These properties facilitate faster ion diffusion and more favorable adsorption/desorption dynamics of reaction intermediates. Moreover, the disordered network can dynamically adjust to varying redox states, a critical feature for multistep processes such as the oxygen evolution and hydrogen evolution reactions [61]. As a result, the amorphous domains serve as highly reactive centers that synergistically operate with the LSC and N-CDs bridge to achieve superior OER and HER performance.

The enhancement of electrocatalytic activity was investigated using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) under a potential of 0.55 V vs. Ag/AgCl. Nyquist plots of LSC, FeOOH, LSC-FeOOH, and LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH were obtained to evaluate the charge transfer resistance (Rct) between the catalysts on the glassy carbon electrode and the KOH electrolyte. It was clearly observed that the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composites exhibited the smallest Rct among all catalysts, indicating more efficient electron transfer between LSC and FeOOH through the N-CDs bridge, which enhanced the conductivity of FeOOH. The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) was also assessed by measuring the electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) at different scan rates, as shown in Fig. 5f. The current densities were normalized by the ECSA or catalyst mass to calculate the specific or mass activity at a potential of 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl. The LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composite exhibited a Cdl of 0.84 mF/cm2, which is more than 1.2 times higher than that of the other catalysts, suggesting that the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composite has a greater number of active sites.

A long-term stability test of LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composites was conducted at a current density of 500 mA/cm2, as shown in Fig. 6. The test was performed using a two-electrode setup with LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH catalysts coated onto Ni foam serving as both anodes and cathodes (Fig. 6a). The cell voltage remained stable at approximately 2.6 V over 230 hours without any noticeable changes, as shown in Fig. 6a. Notably, significant amounts of oxygen and hydrogen continued to be generated at the same voltage even after 230 hours, as confirmed by the inset in Fig. 6a and Supplementary Video 1. In comparison, the IrO2 catalyst exhibited a 10 % voltage increase, rising from an initial 3.5 V to 3.85 V after 72 hours of operation under the same conditions. SEM analysis of the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH catalyst after 230 hours of continuous testing at 500 mA/cm2 revealed no significant peeling or degradation of the coating on the Ni foam (Fig. 6b). This suggests both enhanced electrocatalytic performance and structural stability during the test, as no decline in electrochemical performance was observed. LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH demonstrates superior electrocatalytic activity among perovskite-based catalysts, particularly at high current densities and over long-term operation (Fig. 6c, Table 1 in Supplementary materials) [62–68]. Furthermore, XPS analysis after the long-term stability test showed no significant peak shifts in the La 3d, Fe 2p, or O 1 s spectra, confirming that the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composite remains chemically stable under prolonged high-current-density operation (Fig. 6d). These results collectively establish LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH as a highly stable and efficient catalyst for electrochemical applications.
Supplementary material related to this article can be found online at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2025.111170.

The following is the Supplementary material related to this article Video S1.
Video S1

In conclusion, the LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH composite demonstrates exceptional electrocatalytic performance and stability, making it a promising candidate for oxygen evolution reaction and hydrogen evolution reaction applications. The synergistic combination of LSC, FeOOH, and N-doped carbon dots achieved through optimized wet ball milling enhances electron transfer, structural integrity, and active site availability. The introduction of N-CDs facilitates interfacial charge transport and induces lattice distortions in FeOOH, promoting a transition to amorphous regions that improve catalytic activity. Electrochemical testing showed that the 20 wt% N-CDs composite displayed the lowest overpotentials, reduced Tafel slopes, and enhanced performance at both low and high current densities. Additionally, long-term stability testing at a current density of 500 mA/cm2 confirmed outstanding durability, with no significant structural or chemical degradation over 230 hours. These findings highlight the potential of LSC-N-CDs-FeOOH as a highly efficient and stable electrocatalyst for sustainable energy conversion technologies.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Sang Heon Kim is a combined Master/Ph.D candidate in School of Advanced Materials Science and Engineering at Sungkyunkwan University (SKKU). His current research mainly focused on bifunctional electrocatalysts for oxygen evolution reaction and hydrogen evolution reaction.

Ji Young Park is a Ph.D. candidate under the supervision of Prof. Jeong Min Baik at School of Advanced Materials Science and Engineering at Sungkyunkwan University (SKKU). Her current research is thermoelectric generator, including highly sustainable device for energy harvsting.

Sung Yeol Choi is a combined Master/Ph.D candidate in School of Advanced Materials Science and Engineering at Sungkyunkwan University (SKKU). His current research mainly focused on Material synthesis and application to environmental pollutant valorization by catalysis applications.

Jeongeun Mo is a combined M.S./Ph.D. candidate in the School of Advanced Materials Science and Engineering at Sungkyunkwan University (SKKU), Korea. She received her B.S. degree in Chemical Engineering from SKKU in 2023. Her current research focuses on oxide semiconductors for infrared sensors, particularly for microbolometer applications.

Dr. Hyun-Cheol Song is a principal research scientist in Korea Institute of Science and Technology (KIST) after joined in 2006. He received the B.S. and M.S. degree in Materials Science and Engineering from Korea University, 2004 and 2006, respectively. In 2017, he received Ph.D. in the same field from Virginia Tech. His research interests are now on, piezoelectric and triboelectric energy harvesting, MEMS devices, nanostructured piezoelectric materials, textured piezoelectric ceramics, piezocatalyst, and magneto-thermoelectric generators.

Dr. Chungsuk Choi is an Assistant Professor at Sungkyunkwan University (SKKU), affiliated with the SKKU Advanced Institute of Nanotechnology and the Department of Nanoengineering. He earned his M.S. degree from KAIST and his Ph.D. from UCLA, specializing in the Department of Materials Science and Engineering. Dr. Choi’s research group is focused on advancing the concept of "closed-loop recycling." This innovative approach aims to capture environmental pollutants from air (e.g., CO2) and water (e.g., nitrates) and convert them, along with abundant molecules such as N2, O2, and H2O, into fuels or essential chemical building blocks. However, the development of this technology is hindered by challenges such as ineffective pollutant capture systems and the limitations of current catalyst materials. By leveraging renewable energy sources, such as electricity or sunlight, through electrocatalytic and photocatalytic reactions, his team seeks to enable sustainable closed-loop recycling. Their research integrates expertise from materials science, chemistry, environmental science, quantum mechanics, and cutting-edge in-situ characterization techniques to deepen the understanding of functional materials for energy and environmental sustainability.

Dr. Hyesung Park is currently an Associate Professor in Department of Integrative Energy Engineering, College of Engineering, Korea University, South Korea. He received his M.S. and Ph.D. degrees in Mechanical Engineering and Electrical Engineering and Computer Science from Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) in 2007 and 2012. He was a postdoctoral associate at MIT from 2012 to 2013 and postdoctoral fellow at Northwestern University in 2014. His research interests focus on synthesis of low-dimensional materials such as graphene, transition metal dichalcogenides, and nanowires, and their applications into functional devices in nanoelectronics, nanophotonics, energy harvesting, and bioelectronics.

Dr. Jeong Min Baik is Professor in School of Advanced Materials Science and Engineering at Sungkyunkwan University (SKKU). He received his M.S. and Ph.D. from Pohang University in Department of Materials Science and Engineering in 2001 and 2006. His recent research interest is focused on the synthesis of nanomaterials and nanostructures such as nanoparticles, nanowires, nanolayers, and nanopores for the applications of Energy-Conversion Devices and Nano-photonic Devices. Particular interests are concerned with the development of Piezoelectric/Triboelectric Nanogenerators and Artificial Photosynthesis.
